description: BiasGuard Bias Pattern Knowledge Base — 200+ indicators across 8 categories

GENDER_BIAS.description: Language that signals preference for a specific gender or uses gendered stereotypes
GENDER_BIAS.eeoc_reference: Title VII of Civil Rights Act 1964 — Sex Discrimination

patterns.explanation: Male-coded term that reduces female applicant pool by up to 18% (Gaucher et al., 2011)
Male-coded language that discourages women from applying
Male-coded term with lower appeal to female applicants
Male-coded adjective linked to in-group favoritism in hiring research
Male-coded; applied negatively to women displaying same trait (double-bind bias)
Masculine-coded when used without context; borderline
Explicitly gendered; excludes non-male workers
Gendered job title; use gender-neutral form
Gendered role title
Female-coded stereotype; implies gender expectations for roles
Male-coded trait; women who are competitive often penalized
Binary gender assumption; excludes non-binary candidates
Binary gender framing in communications
May signal gender-specific leave concerns; use parental
Male-coded leadership term
Male-coded when contrasted with collaborative in JD context
Gendered cultural archetype; male default
Male-coded; women less likely to apply to roles requiring 'genius' (Leslie et al., 2015)
Similar to genius; female-is-ordinary implicit association
Male-default leadership prototype assumption

AGE_BIAS.description: Language that favors younger candidates or disadvantages older workers
AGE_BIAS.eeoc_reference: Age Discrimination in Employment Act 1967 (ADEA) — protects workers 40+

patterns.explanation: Direct age reference. Violates ADEA when used as qualification criteria
Code word for young/age preference; implies older workers aren't energetic
Code for born post-~1990; directly discriminatory under ADEA
Effectively limits applicants to those in their 20s. Often ADEA violation
Equivalent to 'recent graduate' — age discriminatory
Often used as age code; implies veterans are too comfortable
Implies early-career preference
Frequently used as proxy for 'too old'; discriminatory in many jurisdictions
neutral_alternatives: We welcome candidates with advanced experience

patterns.explanation: Coded language for preferring younger candidates
Requesting graduation year allows age calculation; direct ADEA risk
Sometimes used as age code, but often genuinely descriptive
When used as cultural proxy can disadvantage older workers
Implies age-linked technology comfort assumptions
Graduation class year enables age discrimination in screening
Pejorative framing of established practices; age-coded
Targeting specific older technologies to screen out experienced workers
Low risk but sometimes used to imply youth preference
Can imply youth bias but often genuinely describes the work
Maximum experience caps directly target older workers (e.g., '0-3 years only')
neutral_alternatives: entry-level role; all experience levels evaluated

patterns.explanation: Often age-coded; assumes lifestyle alignment of younger workers
neutral_alternatives: we balance high performance with team wellbeing

RACIAL_ETHNIC_BIAS.description: Language that disadvantages candidates based on race, ethnicity, or national origin

patterns.explanation: Most commonly cited proxy for racial in-group preference. Highly correlated with homogeneity (Rivera, 2012)
Discriminates on national origin; illegal unless genuinely required for the role
National origin discrimination; accents are legally protected characteristics
When applied selectively, signals low expectation for Black candidates (racial microaggression)
Same as 'articulate' — frequently a racial microaggression
Socioeconomic and racial proxy; elite institutions are disproportionately white and wealthy
Proxy for socioeconomic and racial exclusion
Othering language that marks non-white candidates as foreign or unusual
Can screen out immigrant communities and those from lower-income areas
neutral_alternatives: on-site role in [city]; relocation support available

patterns.explanation: Positive evolution from 'culture fit' but still requires specificity to be non-discriminatory
neutral_alternatives: we seek candidates who expand our team's perspectives (specify how)

patterns.explanation: Appropriative term with colonial connotations
Appropriation of Indigenous spiritual practice; deeply offensive
Othering language; commonly used in racially coded contexts
Classic national origin microaggression; implies candidate is not 'really' from stated location
Low risk as terminology but whiteboard-only interviews can disadvantage neurodiverse candidates

DISABILITY_BIAS.description: Language that discriminates based on physical or mental disability
DISABILITY_BIAS.eeoc_reference: Americans with Disabilities Act 1990 (ADA) — Disability Discrimination

patterns.explanation: Direct disability discrimination unless a bona fide occupational requirement
neutral_alternatives: able to perform essential functions with or without accommodation

patterns.explanation: Inaccessible for candidates with mobility impairments; not ADA compliant
neutral_alternatives: interviews available in accessible formats; please contact us

patterns.explanation: Can disadvantage candidates with learning disabilities; specify what learning is needed
neutral_alternatives: demonstrates ability to acquire new skills

patterns.explanation: Can screen out candidates with chronic illness or fatigue conditions

patterns.term: must be able to stand for 8 hours
patterns.explanation: Only include physical requirements if genuinely essential; ADA requires accommodation consideration
neutral_alternatives: role may require extended periods standing; accommodations available

patterns.explanation: Direct ADA violation; cannot require perfect health
neutral_alternatives: ability to perform essential functions with or without reasonable accommodation

patterns.explanation: Legal in most jurisdictions but can discriminate against those with legal prescriptions; ensure policy is clear
neutral_alternatives: pre-employment background screening; accommodations for legal prescriptions

patterns.explanation: Ableist language stigmatizing mental health conditions
Ableist slang; originally referred to mobility impairment
Originally referred to speech impairment; now ableist slang
Can disadvantage ADHD candidates unless genuinely essential; frame as specific task
Can stigmatize mental health conditions; implies those with conditions are not viable
Implies a standard that excludes neurodiverse or disabled candidates

SOCIOECONOMIC_BIAS.description: Language that disadvantages candidates from lower socioeconomic backgrounds

patterns.explanation: Excludes candidates who cannot afford to work without pay; entrenches class inequality
Explicit class filter; correlates with race, socioeconomic status, and legacy admissions
Same as elite university; excludes qualified candidates from non-ranked institutions
Excludes urban, lower-income candidates who rely on transit
Class-coded benefit or expectation; implies expensive leisure norms
Class-coded presentation standard; disadvantages first-generation professionals
Referral hiring disadvantages candidates without professional networks (often class/race correlated)
neutral_alternatives: open application process; referrals welcome alongside direct applications

patterns.explanation: Class-coded activity often expected of candidates; excludes lower-income applicants

APPEARANCE_BIAS.description: Language that discriminates based on physical appearance

patterns.explanation: Creates opportunity for appearance, race, age, and gender discrimination
neutral_alternatives: no photo required; evaluated on qualifications only

patterns.explanation: Vague standard that often reflects racial or class-coded grooming norms
Appearance discrimination; illegal in many jurisdictions
Often racially coded (natural hair styles, etc.); ensure policy is specific and equitable
neutral_alternatives: follows our professional dress code [link to policy]

patterns.explanation: Vague appearance standard with potential for subjective discrimination
neutral_alternatives: adheres to professional standards as defined in our dress code

COGNITIVE_STYLE_BIAS.description: Language that disadvantages neurodiverse candidates or assumes neurotypical processing styles

patterns.explanation: Can disadvantage ADHD and neurodiverse candidates; specify actual task requirements
Fine if accurate, but should be specific to the role's actual requirements
Disadvantages introverts and those with social anxiety; specify interaction requirements
Vague; some neurodiverse candidates may interpret idioms literally
Neurotypical social expression standard; disadvantages introverts and autistic candidates

INTERVIEW_BIAS.description: Biased questions or patterns observed in interview transcripts

patterns.explanation: Illegal pre-employment inquiry in all US states; implies commitment concern for women
neutral_alternatives: Are you able to meet the travel/schedule requirements of this role?

patterns.explanation: EEOC prohibited inquiry; implies stability bias against single candidates or concern for women
National origin discrimination; prohibited inquiry
neutral_alternatives: Are you authorized to work in [country]?

patterns.explanation: Direct ADEA violation in hiring context
Religious discrimination; Title VII protected characteristic
Marital status probe; implies financial dependency assumptions
ADA prohibited pre-offer inquiry
neutral_alternatives: Can you perform the essential functions of this role with or without reasonable accommodation?

patterns.explanation: Medical inquiry; ADA violation pre-offer
Citizenship status can be a proxy for national origin discrimination; ask about work authorization instead
neutral_alternatives: Are you authorized to work in the United States?

patterns.explanation: Opens door to prohibited inquiries about marital status, children, religion
neutral_alternatives: Tell me about a time you balanced competing priorities at work

patterns.explanation: Prohibited in 21 US states; perpetuates gender pay gap
neutral_alternatives: What are your compensation expectations for this role?

patterns.explanation: Can reveal national origin, socioeconomic status, or race
neutral_alternatives: remove unless directly relevant; ask about professional background instead

sources: Gaucher, D., Friesen, J., & Kay, A.C. (2011). Evidence that gendered wording in job advertisements exists and sustains gender inequality.
Rivera, L.A. (2012). Hiring as cultural matching: The case of elite professional service firms.
Leslie, S.J., Cimpian, A., Meyer, M., & Freeland, E. (2015). Expectations of brilliance underlie gender distributions across academic disciplines.
Bertrand, M., & Mullainathan, S. (2004). Are Emily and Greg more employable than Lakisha and Jamal?
